coredata.dc:title: Design and fabrication of new anticancer sensor for monitoring of daunorubicin using 1-methyl-3-octylimidazolinium chloride and tin oxide/nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot nanocomposite electrochemical sensor
coredata.dc:creator: Zare, Najmeh
Karimi-Maleh, Hassan
Moghaddam, Mojtaba Saei
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a novel tin oxide/nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot nanocomposite (SnO2-NDGQD) and 1-methyl-3-octylimidazolinium chloride (1M3OICl) ionic liquid amplified carbon paste electrode (CPE) was fabricated as an efficient and fast-response sensor to determine daunorubicin, an anticancer drug. The electrochemical characteristics of daunorubicin at the surface of the 1M3OICl/SnO2-NDGQD/CPE was explored via various voltammetric methods. The high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) images were recorded to examine the morphological structure of the as-synthesized nanocomposites. The 1M3OICl/SnO2-NDGQD/CPE offered a wide linear concentration of 0.001–280.0 μM with a low detection limit of 0.40 nM at the optimized experimental conditions using square wave voltammetric (SWV) method. In a nutshell, the developed electrode illustrated outstanding selectivity in the presence of interfering agents and long-term stability. The1M3OICl/SnO2-NDGQD/CPE was used as new and powerful analytical tool for determination of daunorubicin in real samples with recovery range 98.75%–104.8%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Daunorubicin sensing
Anticancer drug sensing
1-methyl-3-octylimidazolinium chloride
Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis method of tin oxide/nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot nanocomposite
Investigation of the effect of 1M3OICl and SnO2-NDGQD in the modification process

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HR-TEM image for tin oxide/nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot.

A) Oxidation current of daunorubicin at the surface of CPE modified with different percentage of SnO2-NDGQD. B) Oxidation current of daunorubicin at the surface of CPE modified with different percentage of 1M3OICl.

CVs a) CPE in the presence of 700 μM daunorubicin; b) SnO2-NDGQD/CPE in the presence of 700 μM daunorubicin; c) 1M3OICl/CPE in the presence of 700 μM daunorubicin, and d) 1M3OICl/SnO2-NDGQD/CPE in the presence 700 μM daunorubicin.

pH-potential curve for electro-oxidation of 600 μM danurobosin at the surface of 1M3OICl/SnO2-NDGQD/CPE. Inset) Relative cyclic voltammograms for electro-oxidation of 600 μM danurobosin at the surface of 1M3OICl/SnO2-NDGQD/CPE.

The plot of anodic current vs. square root of scan rate of daunorubicin anticancer drug on the surface of 1M3OICl/SnO2-NDGQD/CPE. Inset) cyclic voltammograms of 500 μM daunorubicin at different scan rates of 10, 20, 40, 60, 100, 200, 250 and 400 mV in 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution, pH 7.0.

The plot of anodic current vs. scan rate of daunorubicin anticancer drug on the surface of 1M3OICl/SnO2-NDGQD/CPE. Inset) cyclic voltammograms of 500 μM daunorubicin at different scan rates of 10, 20, 40, 60, 100, 200, 250 and 400 mV in 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution, pH 7.0.
Tafel plot of 500 μM daunorubicin at surface of 1M3OICl/SnO2-NDGQD/CPE.
Chronoamprograms of the 1M3OICl/SnO2-NDGQD/CPE in the presence of a) 100 μM, b) 200 μM, and c) 300 μM daunorubicin. Inset) Relative Cottrell plots.

The plot of daunorubicin oxidation current vs. daunorubicin concentration at the surface of 1M3OICl/SnO2-NDGQD/CPE. Inset) SWVs of daunorubicin in the concentration range of 0.01–280.0 μM at the surface of 1M3OICl/SnO2-NDGQD/CPE.
The diagram of daunorubicin oxidation current vs. day at the surface of 1M3OICl/SnO2-NDGQD/CPE.
Elemental analysis of nitrogen-doped quantum dot graphene nanocomposite placed on tin oxide.
Comparison of proposed electrochemical sensors for monitoring of daunorubicin.

row.entry: nitrogen-doped reduced graphene oxide and single walled carbon nanotubes, and then loaded with platinum nanoparticles

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Selectivity results for monitoring of 10 μM daunorubicin at a surface of 1M3OICl/SnO2-NDGQD/CPE.
Determination of daunorubicin using 1M3OICl/SnO2-NDGQD/CPE in real samples.

head.ce:title: Design and fabrication of new anticancer sensor for monitoring of daunorubicin using 1-methyl-3-octylimidazolinium chloride and tin oxide/nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot nanocomposite electrochemical sensor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering and Energy, Quchan University of Technology, Quchan, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Engineering and Energy

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering and Energy, Quchan University of Technology, Quchan, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Sustainable Engineering, Saveetha School of Engineering, SIMATS, Chennai, 602105, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Sustainable Engineering, Saveetha School of Engineering, SIMATS, Chennai, India 602105

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemical Engineering and Energy, Quchan University of Technology, Quchan, Iran.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Engineering and Energy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a novel tin oxide/nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot nanocomposite (SnO2-NDGQD) and 1-methyl-3-octylimidazolinium chloride (1M3OICl) ionic liquid amplified carbon paste electrode (CPE) was fabricated as an efficient and fast-response sensor to determine daunorubicin, an anticancer drug. The electrochemical characteristics of daunorubicin at the surface of the 1M3OICl/SnO2-NDGQD/CPE was explored via various voltammetric methods. The high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) images were recorded to examine the morphological structure of the as-synthesized nanocomposites. The 1M3OICl/SnO2-NDGQD/CPE offered a wide linear concentration of 0.001–280.0 μM with a low detection limit of 0.40 nM at the optimized experimental conditions using square wave voltammetric (SWV) method. In a nutshell, the developed electrode illustrated outstanding selectivity in the presence of interfering agents and long-term stability. The1M3OICl/SnO2-NDGQD/CPE was used as new and powerful analytical tool for determination of daunorubicin in real samples with recovery range 98.75%–104.8%.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is a disease in which some cells in the body grow uncontrollably and spread to other parts of the body (Nourazarian et al., 2014). It may start almost anywhere in the human body, which includes billions of cells. Human cells regularly grow and replicate to produce new cells as needed by the body (Culotta and Koshland, 1992). Cells die as they get old or injured, and new cells replace them. Chemotherapy and the use of anticancer medicines are widely recognized as the cornerstones of treatment for most malignancies (Florea and Büsselberg, 2011). As a result, much research is being undertaken to determine the efficacy, side effects, and impacts of anticancer drug concentrations on the derma process, as well as the combined effect of anticancer medications in the chemotherapeutic process. As a consequence, work in this field is essential for the worldwide health industry.

Injectable daunorubicin was launched as a chemotherapeutic treatment for many diseases, including chronic and acute myelogenous leukemia and breast cancer (Jiang et al., 2003). Daunorubicin, which is widely recommended in conjunction with other anticancer medications in chemotherapy, such as tamoxifen, is used to prevent the adverse effects of surgery and the risk of breast cancer reinfection (Guo and Lu, 2010). Numerous studies have been undertaken to investigate the effects and benefits of taking daunorubicin and tamoxifen at the same time on the human body. As a result, controlling the dosage of this medicine during the chemotherapy procedure is critical. Several analytical sensors were proposed many years ago for daunorubicin determination (Jiang et al., 2003; Hulhoven and Desager, 1977; Karimi-Maleh et al., 2021; Katari and Ajitha, 2022).

Most of these treatments, however, are limited in their use due to a variety of limitations such as expensive and sophisticated apparatus, substantial technical competence, and time-consuming. Electrochemical methods have become more acceptable and appealing because of their quick reaction, high sensitivity, cheap cost, and in-situ monitoring (Baghayeri and Veisi, 2015; Karimi et al., 2020, 2022; Alizadeh et al., 2022a, 2022b; Orooji et al., 2021; Sadeghi et al., 2020). Since various attempts have been made to create and construct new, more sensitive, and selective developed sensors, electrochemical technologies have been frequently exploited for this goal (Hussain et al., 2022; Khand et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2022; Zhao et al., 2022; Sebokolodi et al., 2022; Salmanpour et al., 2018). In terms of electrical conductivity and selectivity, nanomaterials and ionic liquids are two major options for modifying electrochemical sensors (Babu et al., 2021; Asrami et al., 2017; Cheraghi et al., 2022; Ahmadzadeh et al., 2017; Ghalkhani et al., 2022). Nanomaterials and especially nanocomposites showed many advantages in different branch of science and technology (Khan et al., 2021; Ali et al., 2022; Peiravi and Alinejad, 2021). The unique properties of nanomaterials have created different applications for it, which has created a tremendous revolution in the world of science (Kalhor et al., 2021; Cao et al., 2021; Zhao et al., 2021; Kurnaz Yetim et al., 2021; Babudurai et al., 2021; Mansur et al., 2022; Kazemi et al., 2021). Unique thermal, catalytic, mechanical and electrical properties have been observed in nanomaterials (Wu et al., 2021; Narasimharao and Malkhasian, 2022; Gharib and Arab, 2022; John et al., 2021; Kianfar et al., 2021; Mpelane et al., 2022; Hosseinzadeh et al., 2021; Alidadykhoh et al., 2021). One of these features is high electrical conductivity, which can be used in making electrochemical sensors (Moghaddam et al., 2021; Peyman et al., 2021; Ebrahimi and Beitollahi, 2021). On the other hand, current potential systems' high capability may be boosted by utilizing sensors modified with nanomaterials, ionic liquids, or a combination of the two (Fouladgar, 2015, 2018; Nasehi et al., 2022; Fouladgar and Karimi-Maleh, 2013).

The current study is the first one to show how to synthesize and utilize a tin oxide/nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot nanocomposite to fabricate a unique and sensitive CPE modified sensor in the presence of ionic liquid and paraffin as binders. This novel electrode demonstrated high performance as an effective sensor for determining daunorubicin in real samples, with low detection limits, reasonable linear ranges, and outstanding selectivity using voltammetry for the first time.

Daunorubicin hydrochloride (90.0%), Graphite powder (≥98.0%), paraffin oil (99.0%), ethylenediamine (≥98.0%), and L-glutamic acid (99.0%) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich Company (Germany), whereas the other materials were acquired from Merck Company (Analytical grade).

The synthesized composites were characterized by HR-TEM. Electrochemical measurements including the cyclic voltammetry (CV), chronoamperometry (CA), and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), were recorded via Ivium vertex potensiostat/galvanostat. The Ag/AgCl/KCl (Azar electrode Company) and Pt wire were used as references and counter electrodes in this work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis method of tin oxide/nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the graphene quantum dot was synthesized using the following method. The first step was to dissolve 2 g of L-glutamic acid and 2 ml of ethylenediamine in 40 ml of double-distilled water and stir for 15 min. The prepared sample was transferred to an autoclave and exposed to 180 °C for 6 h. Afterward, the sample was allowed to cool to the ambient temperature and then filtered. Finally, a yellow solution was obtained, which confirmed the formation of the nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot.

In the second step, tin oxide nanoparticles were synthesized by the following procedure. For this purpose, tin chloride solution (1.5 M) + 10 ml of double-distilled was mixed and stirred for 20 min. Following, the CTAB solution with a concentration of 15 mM was added to the sample, and the pH of the sample was adjusted to 8.0 using sodium hydride solution (0.9 M). The sample was stirred over 10 min, and the tin hydroxide sample was centrifuged and separated. In continuous, the tin hydroxide sample was washed several times with water/ethanol solution and dried at 60 °C. The sample was then calcined at 600 °C for 6 h in an oven.

Finally, for the synthesis of tin oxide/nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot nanocomposite, 0.3 g of tin-oxide nanoparticles were immersed in 15 ml of a solution of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot and ultra-sonicated for 1 h. The sample was then placed in an autoclave at 60 °C for 12 h, and the composite was synthesized.

0.85 g of graphite powder +0.15 g of SnO2-NDGQD was mixed into the glass mortar in the presence of 10 ml ethanol as solvent. After the evaporation of ethanol in the 40 OC, the paraffin oil and 1M3OICl with a ratio of 8:2 (v/v) were added in a glass mortar and hand-mixed over 45 min. The 1M3OICl/SnO2-NDGQD paste was transferred to the end of glass tubes in the presence of coper wire and employed as the working electrode.

Fig. 1 showed the HR-TEM of tin oxide/nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot nanocomposite. The presence of spherical tin oxide nanoparticles and its boundary distance with nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot was well defined that graphene quantum dot was located around spherical nanoparticles. The results of elemental analysis of synthetic tin oxide/nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot were shown in Table 1 . The presence of nitrogen confirmed doping of this element in graphene quantum dot structure. On the other hand, the presence of C, Sn, and O confirmed the synthesis of tin oxide/nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot with good purity.

To fabricate a powerful electroanalytical sensor for measuring daunorubicin, the ration of SnO2-NDGQD to graphite powder, and a ratio of 1M3OICl to paraffin oils (as binders) was optimized in 1M3OICl/SnO2-NDGQD/CPE matrix. An oxidation signal of 700 μM daunorubicin was recorded at the surface of CPE modified with different percentages of SnO2-NDGQD.

As can be seen in Fig. 2 A, with an increasing percentage of SnO2-NDGQD to 10% (w/w) in the carbon paste matrix, the oxidation signal of daunorubicin was increased. After this value, the oxidation current of daunorubicin remained constant that confirmed the modification of the electrode completed and the electrode surface reached the maximum electrical conductivity. On the other hand, the ratio of 1M3OICl to paraffin oil as a binder in the fabrication of the modified sensor was optimized. For this goal, an oxidation signal of 700 μM daunorubicin was recorded at the surface of CPE modified with the different percentages of 1M3OICl as a binder. As can be seen in Fig. 2B, with the increasing percentages of 1M3OICl to 20% (v/v) in the carbon paste matrix, the oxidation signal of daunorubicin was increased. After this value, the oxidation current of daunorubicin was decreased. This is due to the accumulation of a viscous layer of ionic liquid on the electrode surface and reduced conductivity of the electrode surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the effect of 1M3OICl and SnO2-NDGQD in the modification process
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 showed the CVs of the CPE in the modification process in the presence of 700 μM of daunorubicin under optimal test conditions. The results offered the oxidation currents of 11.76 μA, 22.63 μA 30.2 μA, and 63.48 μA for the daunorubicin oxidation signal on the surface of CPE (curve a), SnO2-NDGQD/CPE (curve b), 1M3OICl/CPE (curve c) and 1M3OICl/SnO2-NDGQD/CPE (curve d), respectively. Also, the oxidation potential of 700 μM daunorubicin was observed at the potentials of 420 mV, and 380 mV on the surface of a simple carbon paste electrode, and 1M3OICl/SnO2-NDGQD/CPE, respectively. The increasing about 5.4 in oxidation current and decreasing about 40 mV in oxidation potential of daunorubicin with modification of CPE with 1M3OICl and SnO2-NDGQD, confirmed the powerful ability of these two mediators for fabrication of highly sensitive electroanalytical sensor for monitoring of daunorubicin.

Since daunorubicin is an electroactive phenolic anticancer drug with two active phenolic sides, it is expected that the electrochemical response of this drug depends on pH changes. Due to this, the pH conditions were initially optimized for electrochemical analysis of the drug daunorubicin. In this regard, to obtain the best electrochemical condition in the monitoring system of daunorubicin, the cyclic voltammogram of 600 μM daunorubicin on the surface of 1M3OICl/SnO2-NDGQD/CPE was recorded in the pH range 5.0–9.0 (Fig. 4 ).

The results showed that the oxidation potential of daunorubicin at the surface of 1M3OICl/SnO2-NDGQD/CPE shifted to the lower positive values with increasing pH with an equation Ep = −0.66 pH + 0.847 (r2 = 0.994) that indicating the number of electrons and protons exchanged in this electrochemical process of daunorubicin was equal. According to obtaining results, the mechanism of Scheme 1 was suggested for the electro-oxidation of daunorubicin in aqueous solution.

Moreover, the maximum sensitivity for monitoring of daunorubicin at the surface of 1M3OICl/SnO2-NDGQD/CPE was observed at pH 7.0, and these conditions were recommended for measuring and working with this sensor.

The electrochemical behavior of daunorubicin was investigated at the different scan rates on the surface of 1M3OICl/SnO2-NDGQD/CPE by the CV method (Fig. 5 ). The linear relation was observed between the oxidation current of daunorubicin anticancer drug and υ1/2 with an equation of I = 4.7151 υ1/2–5.6024 (r2 = 0.9958) that indicated diffusion-controlled process for redox reaction of the daunorubicin anticancer drug on the surface of by the diffusion procedure.

Moreover, the plot of the anodic current of daunorubicin anticancer drug versus scan rate was depicted in Fig. 6 . As can be seen, the data was out of linear mode, which confirmed that the process was not under the adsorption control.

The Tafel plot of 1M3OICl/SnO2-NDGQD/CPE in the presence of 500 μM daunorubicin showed in Fig. 7 . According to slope of this figure and Tafel equation, the value of α was calculated about 0.54 that confirm quasi-reversible behavior for redox reaction daunorubicin at surface of 1M3OICl/SnO2-NDGQD/CPE.

After confirming the diffusion process of the daunorubicin anticancer drug at the 1M3OICl/SnO2-NDGQD/CPE, the value of the diffusion coefficient can be determined by Chronoamperometric investigation (Fig. 8 ). For this goal, chronoamprograms of the 1M3OICl/SnO2-NDGQD/CPE were recorded in the presence of 100 μM, 200 μM, and 300 μM daunorubicin using applied potential 500 mV for 30 s. Using obtaining curves and recording Cottrell plots of them (Fig. 8 inset), the value of the diffusion coefficient of daunorubicin at the surface of 1M3OICl/SnO2-NDGQD/CPE was calculated at about D = 0.49*10−6 cm2/s using Cottrell equation.

Selectivity of 1M3OICl/SnO2-NDGQD/CPE for monitoring the daunorubicin is recognized as an essential factor in analytical measurements. The ability of the sensor to detect and measure the species without interference is a great help in obtaining accurate information for researchers to investigate. The selectivity results for monitoring of 10 μM daunorubicin at a surface of 1M3OICl/SnO2-NDGQD/CPE were reported in Table 3 with the accepted relative standard error of less than 5%. The results confirmed the selectivity of 1M3OICl/SnO2-NDGQD/CPE for sensing daunorubicin.

Moreover, the stability of 1M3OICl/SnO2-NDGQD/CPE as a newly developed electrode was investigated in this step. In this regard, the daunorubicin signal was recorded on different days under the same conditions. As can be seen in Fig. 10 , after 90 days, the 1M3OICl/SnO2-NDGQD/CPE showed a 90% oxidation signal of daunorubicin that was observed on the first day. This point confirmed 3-month stability of 1M3OICl/SnO2-NDGQD/CPE as an analytical tool for monitoring the daunorubicin.

Injection and dextrose solution were used as real samples for the monitoring of daunorubicin using 1M3OICl/SnO2-NDGQD/CPE as an electro-analytical tool. The concentration of daunorubicin was determined by the standard addition method and obtaining data displayed in Table 4 . As can be seen, an acceptable recovery range (98.75%–104.8%) was detected for sensing of daunorubicin using 1M3OICl/SnO2-NDGQD/CPE as analytical tool.

In this work, a selective and acceptable electrochemical system was designed for the determination of daunorubicin in biological fluids. For this goal, the CPE was amplified with 1M3OICl/SnO2 and NDGQD. The 1M3OICl/SnO2-NDGQD showed good catalytic activity for sensing and monitoring daunorubicin in real samples. The developed electrode detected daunorubicin with a limit of 0.4 nM, and LDR of 0.001–280 μM. Furthermore, the great selectivity and stability of the fabricated electrode, together with its ease of production and surface renewal, make it particularly useful for precise detection of daunorubicin in actual samples. The 1M3OICl/SnO2-NDGQD showed long time stability (about 3.0 month) for sensing of daunorubicin in optimum condition. The1M3OICl/SnO2-NDGQD/CPE was used as new and powerful analytical tool for determination of daunorubicin in real samples with recovery range 98.75%–104.8%.

Najmeh Zare and Dr. Mojtaba Saei Moghaddam: Experimental part, Characterization of data and analysis of experimental part. Prof. Hassan Karimi-Maleh: Writing- Original draft preparationand revise of paper.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
